Plant Soil (2021) 465:549–561
https://doi.org/10.1007/s11104-021-05017-5

 REGULAR ARTICLE



Mowing increases fine root production and root turnover
in an artificially restored Songnen grassland
Yan Luo · Xinyu Wang · Meng Cui · Jing Wang ·
Yingzhi Gao




Received: 31 December 2020 / Accepted: 20 May 2021 / Published online: 14 June 2021
© The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2021


Abstract                                                          turnover varied between 1.58 and 3.41 times per year.
Aims Mowing is a common management practice in                    Fine root production, root mortality and root standing
grassland ecosystems. However, how mowing affects                 crop exhibited clear seasonal patterns in both the nat-
fine root dynamics remains poorly explored.                       ural and artificial grasslands, and these were mainly
Methods A field experiment was conducted to study                 driven by precipitation and temperature. Root produc-
how mowing influences root dynamics in artificial                 tion and root turnover responded differently to mow-
and natural grasslands for three consecutive years                ing in different types of grassland. In the artificially
with root-window method.                                          restored grassland, mowing significantly increased
Results Root production among the treatments var-                 root production and root turnover (15.01% and
ied from 312 to 681 m ­m−2 each year and fine root                13.06%, respectively) and decreased the root lifespan
                                                                  by 14.60%. In contrast, none these effects were signif-
                                                                  icant in the naturally restored grassland. The mowing-
Responsible Editor: Tim S. George.                                induced increase in root production and root turnover
Supplementary information The online version
                                                                  in the artificially restored grassland was mainly due
contains supplementary material available at https://​doi.​       to an enhancement in aboveground biomass and soil
org/​10.​1007/​s11104-​021-​05017-5.                              moisture. However, the lack of an effect on these
                                                                  parameters from mowing in the naturally restored
Y. Luo · X. Wang · M. Cui · Y. Gao (*)
                                                                  grassland may be due to the higher species diversity.
Institute of Grassland Science, Key Laboratory
of Vegetation Ecology of the Ministry of Education, Jilin         Conclusions Our findings demonstrate that mowing
Songnen Grassland Ecosystem National Observation                  might accelerate root turnover and improve below-
and Research Station, Northeast Normal University,                ground productivity in the artificially restored grass-
130024 Changchun, China
                                                                  land, highlighting that suitable mowing is an efficient
e-mail: gaoyz108@nenu.edu.cn
                                                                  way to manage artificial grasslands from the perspec-
M. Cui · J. Wang                                                  tive of the dynamics of root system.
State Key Laboratory of Vegetation and Environmental
Change, Institute of Botany, Chinese Academy
                                                                  Keywords Mowing · Grassland type · Fine root
of Sciences, Beijing, China
                                                                  dynamics · Root turnover · Root lifespan
Y. Gao
State Environmental Protection Key Laboratory
of Wetland Ecology and Vegetation Restoration, Northeast
Normal University, Changchun, Jilin Province 130117,
China

Introduction                                                 (Li and Huang 2008). On the other hand, Yu et al.
                                                             (2018) found that soil salinity decreased as the land
Plants allocate approximately 60% of their photosyn-         was converted from the farmland to grassland in
thate for the production and maintenance of roots (Gao       semi-arid agroecosystems. Furthermore, Yu et al.
et al. 2008; Norby and Jackson 2000), and the death and      (2019) also found that soil nutrients in a succes-
decomposition of roots is the primary input of organic       sional regrowth grassland were higher than those
carbon and nutrient into the soil in many ecosystems         in planted grasslands in the upper soil layers, and
(McCormack et al. 2014; Bai et al. 2010). Therefore,         these included SOC and total N, total P, and total K.
root dynamics are among the most important factors for       It has been demonstrated that improvements to soil
carbon balance and nutrient cycling (Matamala et al.         conditions can reduce root turnover and increase
2003). Nonetheless, the inaccessibility of the root sys-     root lifespan (Bai et al. 2008).
tem and the difficulty of measuring root production and          The Songnen grassland used to be one of the most
mortality simultaneous have historically limited our abil-   important forage production areas in northern China.
ity to study fine root turnover.                             However, from 1986 to 2000 more than a quarter of the
    Mowing is an important management in grass-              grassland was developed for arable land to ensure the
land ecosystems and its most direct effect is remov-         rapidly increasing population’s demand for food crops,
ing plant aboveground biomass and changing the               and degradation of the Songnen grassland became
supply of photosynthates (Baoyin et al. 2014). Up to         more and more serious (Liu et al. 2009). The Chinese
now, research in this area has focused on the mow-           government has carried out a series of measures and
ing effects on belowground biomass and net primary           policies to help farmers to implement the policy of
production (Diabate et al. 2018; Xu et al. 2014), how-       returning farmland to grassland in some areas that are
ever, there are few studies on how mowing affects            unsuitable for planting crops, hoping to alleviate grass-
root turnover. In general, mowing increases soil tem-        land degradation and ensure appropriate utilization of
perature and decreases soil water content by reduc-          grassland resources. However, the effects of restoration
ing the aboveground biomass and litter, thus reduc-          from cropland to grassland on root production and root
ing root production and increasing root mortality            turnover are not yet to be quantified in northeastern
and fine root turnover rate (Wei et al. 2019; Liu and        China. In particular, there is a lack of relevant data on
Huang 2002). On the other hand, a large amount of N          root dynamics for the conversion of abandoned farm-
is removed from the grassland ecosystem by cutting,          land to grassland under mowing management.
and this would lead to a decrease in available soil N,           To address this knowledge gap, we established a
resulting in an increasing root turnover rate (Bai et al.    field experiment combing different restoration meas-
2008). These discrepancies in root responses to mow-         ures, including an artificially seeded restored grassland
ing may be due to differences in soil and vegetation         and a naturally restored successional grassland, with
types and variations in land management practices            and without mowing, to evaluate the effects of the dif-
(Gill and Jackson 2000).                                     ferent restoration measures on root production and root
    Previous studies have found that artificial resto-       turnover in abandoned Songnen farmland. The objec-
ration of grassland is one of the quickest and most          tive of this study was to evaluate mowing, seeding and
effective ways to improve root production and eco-           the combined effects on root production and root turno-
logical functions compared to natural grassland              ver in abandoned Songnen farmland.
restoration (Yu et al. 2017a, 2018). However, there
are widely differing responses of root dynamics to
different grassland restoration measures, and these          Materials and methods
are closely related to plant species diversity and
soil conditions (Huang et al. 2019). For example,            Study area
single species plantations of Leymus chinensis in
artificial grasslands had low community stabil-              The experiment site was located at the field station of
ity, and there was greater susceptibility to external        Grassland and Agroecology, the Chinese Academy of
disturbance, thus indirectly affecting root turnover         Sciences, Songnen grassland, Changling County, Jilin

Province, Northeast China (44° 34´N and 123° 30´E,                treatments: naturally restored successional grassland
160 m above sea level). The climate is typical of a               with no mowing (NNM); naturally restored suc-
temperate, semi-arid continental monsoon climate.                 cessional grassland with mowing (NM); artificially
Mean annual precipitation and temperature based on a              seeded restored grassland with no mowing (ANM);
20-year record were 450 mm and 5.6 °C, respectively.              artificially seeded restored grassland with mowing
The precipitation of the three experimental years was                                                               ­ 2.
                                                                  (AM). Each individual plot had an area of 11 × 45 m
strongly fluctuating (Fig. 1). In 2012, the annual pre-           Artificially seeded restored grassland was sown with
cipitation was 525 mm and most of the rain events                 seed of Leymus chinensis (2000 seeds/m2) in May
occurred in June and September. The precipitation                 2011. The dominant species in the naturally restored
in 2013 was 355 mm with a clear seasonal distribu-                successional grassland included Sonchus brachyotus,
tion. The rainfall was relatively low with an annual              Chloris virgata, Chenopodium glaucum and Phrag-
precipitation of 248 mm in 2014, which was identi-                mites communis (Yu et al. 2018). At the end of each
fied as a dry year in this study. The dominating soil             growing season, plants in the mowing treatments
type was meadow solonchaks (comparable to Solon-                  were cut down to 5 cm in height and then the residues
chaks in the FAO/UNESCO taxonomy). The commu-                     were taken away.
nity coverage of the Songnen grassland was 60–90%,
with 100–200 g ­m−2 standing biomass. The dominant
native species were Leymus chinensis, Chloris vir-                Measurements of root parameters
gata, and Puccinellia spp (Yu et al. 2017b).
                                                                  Root dynamics were measured according to protocols
                                                                  described by Bai et al. (2008, 2010). On November 2011,
Experimental design                                               one glass-root window (0.4 cm thick) of 40 cm in length
                                                                  and 28 cm in height was installed vertically into the soil in
The field experiment was established in May 2011 on               each treatment. On each glass window, a 30 cm × 28 cm
abandoned farmland and comprised four blocks. Due                 panel (with 5 cm to the bottom, 3 cm to the top and
to prior continuous cultivation, soil conditions across           10 cm to the right and left sides) was separated into two
the site were similar. In each block, there were four             10 × 10 cm by carving the glass. A vertical profile of




Fig. 1  Monthly precipitation (bars) and mean air temperature (curves) of the experiment site from 2012 to 2014. Values on each
panel are total annual precipitation and mean annual temperature

               Content courtesy of Springer Nature, terms of use apply. Rights reserved.
552                                                                                           Plant Soil (2021) 465:549–561

identical size to the window glass was dug with shovel               The protocols used for root production and root
in each treatment plot. The glass window was put tightly        mortality in this study are mainly based on Burton
against the undisturbed soils and fixed with one iron stick     et al. (2000). Root production was estimated by sum-
at each side. After root-windows were installed, soil was       ming the length of all new roots and adding the exten-
backfilled as tightly as originally. In order to minimize the   sion growth of all previously existing roots. Similarly,
impacts of light and rain on root growth, the upper edge        fine root mortality was determined by summing the
of the glass was installed under the soil surface; further-     length of all died roots during that interval plus the
more, a piece of dark iron (length 20 cm, breadth 1.5 cm        length of existing roots disappeared due to herbi-
and thickness (0.5 mm) was covered on the gap between           vore or dieback. Root production and mortality were
the top glass and the soil. The soil was closely attached to    expressed as root length per glass area observed (m
the glass throughout the whole study period. Observations       ­m−2). The root standing crop was calculated as the
started on 15th June 2012, which was approximately six           seasonal root production minus root mortality accord-
months after installation of the glass root-windows and          ing to Norby et al. (2004). Root production during
lasted until 15th November 2014 with sampling intervals          each sampling period was estimated by summing
of about 15 days in 2012 (from 15 June to 30 September),         the length of all new roots and adding the extension
2013 (from 15 May to 15 October) and 2014 (from 30               growth of all previously existing roots. Annual root
April to 15 November). On each sampling date, the soil           turnover was estimated by dividing cumulative root
on the disturbed side of the glass windows was removed           length production with the average live root length
and the two 10 × 10 cm glass screens were cleaned with a         observed in each growing season according to Bai
soft cloth and tissue paper to obtain clear images. Images       et al. (2010). Fine root lifespan was estimated follow-
were collected with a scanner (BenQ 5000S) connected             ing the methods used by Burton et al. (2000). Root
to a laptop using MiraScan software (6.3, Pitney Bowes           lifespan was defined as the period from first appear-
Mapinfo Corporation, New York, US). The image reso-              ance to disappearance. Root lifespan was evaluated
lution was 300 dpi. Care was taken to maintain the same          by determining the survival of contemporaneously
scanner position for each scan date, and the collected           produced root cohorts from each treatment. Root
images were labeled in chronological order.                      cohorts consisted of all roots produced during the
    The appearance and disappearance of roots were               approximate 15 d sampling interval between two con-
traced and analyzed using MapInfo Professional software          secutive observations, and the survival cohort was
(7.0; Pitney Bowes MapInfo Corporation, New York,                expressed as a fraction of initial cohort root number
NY, USA) as described by Bai et al. (2015). The images           still surviving.
in each chronological sequence in accordance with sam-
pling date, in dealing with every image, were divided into
0–10 cm and 10–20 cm soil profile depths. The lengths           Aboveground biomass collection
of all roots were traced to the nearest 0.01 mm, and each       and measurements
root was given an identification number, being distin-
guished into living or dead roots according to root color       During the three years, plants were harvested when
(Bai et al. 2008). Roots were classified as dead when they      aboveground biomass attained its peak between
appeared dark or were no longer apparent in the profile,        August and September. For each treatment, all the
following the convention of previous minirhizotron stud-        plants within a 1 m × 1 m area per treatment were col-
ies (Cheng et al. 1991; Hendrick and Pregitzer 1993).           lected by cutting and separated and analyzed accord-
All brown and white colored roots were considered               ing to species. The aboveground biomass was con-
as living roots. For the subsequent image sets, the new         sidered approximately equal to the aboveground net
images were overlain with tracings from the previous            primary productivity (ANPP).
dates, allowing previously existing roots to be identified.
Roots that were first observed on the later dates were also
traced, given identification numbers, and designated as         Data analyses
new roots. Complete records were kept for all roots, even
after they were classified as dead, and until they were no      All statistical analysis was performed using SPSS
longer visible.                                                 17.0 software (SPSS Inc, Chicago, USA). Normal

and root standing crop                                     treatment (10.94% and 7.25%, respectively) in the

production, root mortality and root standing crop          significant decrease in root mortality following
in all treatments were distinguished by seasonal           mowing of 13.69% at 0–10 cm, whereas there was
changes, and these patterns were mainly driven by          no significant impact on root mortality at 10–20 cm

production peaked in May and September (Fig. 2a,           respectively) than in 2014 (Fig. 3c, f).
d). Root mortality also displayed a clear seasonal
pattern (Fig. 2b, e). In 2012, root mortality peaked
in July and September in the 0–10 cm soil layer, and       Annual root turnover
August in the 10–20 cm soil layer. In 2013, the peaks

33.33% at 10–20 cm (Table 2). However, the changes                 aboveground biomass was significantly and positively
observed in the naturally restored grassland were not              dependent on root production (r2 = 0.306, P < 0.001)
significant.                                                       and root turnover (r2 = 0.282, P < 0.001) (Fig. 7a, c),

production (r2 = 0.355, P < 0.001) and root turno-                 Discussion
ver (r2 = 0.410, P < 0.001) (Fig. 6a, c), while soil
moisture was negatively correlated with root mor-                  Root dynamics have been considered a critical component
tality (r2 = 0.6787, P < 0.001) (Fig. 6b). In addition,            of ecosystem nutrient dynamics and C sequestration

Fig. 3  Root production,
root mortality and mean
root standing crop (m m ­ −2)
(mean ± SE) of different
treatments across the three
years at the 0–10 cm (a-c)
and 10–20 cm soil depths
(d-f). The different treat-
ments are as indicated in
Fig. 2. The lower-case letters
(a-b-c) and upper-case letters
(A-B-C) represent the sig-
nificant difference (P ≤ 0.05)
between the different treat-
ments and the significant
differences (P ≤ 0.05) across
years, respectively




                                                                                                                  13
               Content courtesy of Springer Nature, terms of use apply. Rights reserved.
556                                                                                                 Plant Soil (2021) 465:549–561

                                                                   dynamic, regardless of the different restoration measures
                                                                   (Fig. 2). Root production peaked in July and September in
                                                                   2012 and 2013, corresponding to the summer and autumn
                                                                   periods, respectively (Fig. 2a, d). The peak for root
                                                                   production in summer could be attributed to synchronous
                                                                   increases in soil water content and temperature. The
                                                                   peak of fine root production in autumn could be
                                                                   explained by the fact that perennial plants need to store
                                                                   enough carbohydrate for survival during the winter and
                                                                   resumption of growth in the following spring (Chiatante
                                                                   et al. 2006; Bai et al. 2015). These results were in line
                                                                   with previous studies undertaken in sugar maple (Morrow
                                                                   1950), northern hardwood forests (Hendrick and Pregitzer
                                                                   1996, 1997; Burton et al. 2000) and the Inner Mongolian
                                                                   steppe (Bai et al. 2015). At the same time, the peaks of
                                                                   root mortality were similar to root production, and it is
                                                                   known that root mortality has a significantly positive
                                                                   correlation with root production (Fig. 2b, e; Bai et al.
                                                                   2008). Nevertheless, the variation in root production and
                                                                   root mortality in 2014 was not as obvious as the first two
                                                                   years (Fig. 2a, b, d and e). This may be due to the high
                                                                   temperature and low precipitation in July and August
                                                                   of that year (Fig. 1). A decrease in soil water content is
                                                                   likely to have limited the release of nutrients into the soil
                                                                   (Gao et al. 2011) and increased soil mechanical resistance
Fig. 4  Annual root turnover (times ­yr−1, mean ± SE) of differ-
                                                                   during root growth, resulting in considerable root death
ent treatments across the three years at the 0–10 cm (a) and
10–20 cm soil depths (b). Treatments and significant differ-       (Fig. 2b, e).
ences (P ≤ 0.05) are as indicated in Fig. 2                           In this study, fine root production varied from 312
                                                                   to 681 m ­m−2, which matched well with the previous
                                                                   studies of Bai et al. (2010, 2015) conducted on the
(Mcsherry and Ritchie 2013), and they are strongly                 Inner Mongolian meadow steppe. However, Wu
affected by precipitation and temperature (Bai et al. 2010).       et al. (2020) found that fine root production varied
Consistent with that contention, our results showed that           from 24.1 to 95.8 m ­m−2 in an alpine meadow on
root production and root mortality had a clear seasonal            the Qinghai-Tibetan Plateau. The reasons underlying


Fig. 5  Survival curves
for cohorts of roots aris-
ing in the 0–10 cm (a)
and 10–20 cm (b) soil
depths within the differ-
ent treatments. The mean
and median lifespan were
calculated via the survival
curves using Kaplan–Meier
analysis. The log-rank test
was performed to compare
the effects of different treat-
ments on the root survival
rate

Table 2  Mean lifespan and median lifespan (days) of roots arising in the 0–10 cm and 10–20 cm soil depths within the different
treatments (Mean ± SE).
Mean lifespan estimates                                                          Median lifespan estimates

Soil Profiles     NNM            NM             ANM               AM             NNM             NM             AM             ANM
0–10 cm           123 ± 3 b      117 ± 4 b      157 ± 10 a        137 ± 4 b      105 ± 3 a       90 ± 3 b       105 ± 5 a      105 ± 3 a
10–20 cm          120 ± 4 b      122 ± 4 b      151 ± 6 a         151 ± 12 a     90 ± 4 c        120 ± 7 a      105 ± 4 b      105 ± 4 b

The different lowercase letters represent the significant difference (P < 0.05) among different treatments within the same soil depth


these differences may be the different soil conditions                  dominated by Kobresia humilis and Festuca ovina
and vegetation types in the two environments (Wang                      etc. (Wang et al. 2012), whose root production is
et al. 2019). The soil type on the Qinghai-Tibetan                      lower than the dominant species in our study (such
Plateau is classified as Mat-Cryic Cambisol (Chinese                    as Leymus chinensis). Root turnover is widely viewed
Soil Taxonomy) with limited N in the surface soil                       as being critical in estimating carbon allocation and
layer, and the region experiences lower annual                          nutrient cycling in grassland ecosystems, and in
mean temperatures and precipitation than the site                       general, the higher the root turnover rate, the greater
in our study, which would inhibit root growth (Lin                      the ability of plants to use nutrients. At the same time,
et al. 2016). Furthermore, the Tibetan grassland is                     root turnover can be affected by many environmental

analysis were conducted to determine the relationship of                tion analysis were conducted to determine the relation-
aboveground biomass to root production (a), root mortality (b)          ship of soil moisture to root production (a), root mortal-
and root turnover (c). For (a): y = 285.65 + 0.44x, r2 = 0.306,         ity (b) and root turnover (c). For (a): y = 316.84 + 12.79x,
P < 0.001; for (b): y = 1073.89 –0.63x, r2 = 0.687, P < 0.001;          r2 = 0.355, P < 0.001; for (b): y = 1278.75 – 31.87x, r2 = 0.687,
for (c): y = 1.47 + 0.002x, r2 = 0.282 P < 0.001                        P < 0.0001; for (c): y = 1.48 + 0.06x, r2 = 0.41 P < 0.0001


                                                                                                                            13
                Content courtesy of Springer Nature, terms of use apply. Rights reserved.
558                                                                                         Plant Soil (2021) 465:549–561

factors including temperature, soil water content and      resulting in the complementary use of soil resources
soil nutrient availabilities, and is especially strongly   (Jacob et al. 2010). In addition, we found that seed-
affected by different measurement methods (Wang            ing decreased root turnover (Fig. 4). This was mainly
et al. 2019; Norby and Jackson 2000). Studies on           because seeding had no significant effect on root
root turnover have mainly focused on root biomass          standing crop (Fig. 3c, f), while it reduced root pro-
by using soil core sampling or the ingrowth donut          duction, leading to a decrease in root turnover. More-
methods as far (Dahlman and Kucera 1965; Shaver            over, the differences in the species between the dif-
and Billings 1975; Fitter et al. 1997). Some studies       ferent treatments is another potential factor affecting
have found root turnover to be 0.33–2.26 times per         root turnover. Gill and Jackson (2000) found that the

that fine roots can be easily lost during separation       the precipitation was lower in the 2014 growing sea-
from the soil, thus underestimating root biomass and       son, which was a dry year (Fig. 1). Water is one of
root turnover (Bai et al. 2015). In addition, using a      the most limiting environmental factors in Songnen
static measuring method such as soil core sampling         grassland, and it strongly affects root growth (Cui
for root production and root turnover at a fixed time is   et al. 2020). Previous studies have shown that the
not convincing because root growth and death occur         numbers of shallow-rooted species reduce as precipi-
simultaneously during the growing season (Pregitzer        tation decreases (Zhang et al. 2019), since shallow-
et al. 1995). The root-window method can avoid             rooted species have greater proportions of surface

and Pregitzer 1992). For this reason, it seems to be       cit led to the death of shallow-rooted annual species
a more powerful tool for estimating root production        and an increase in root mortality (Fig. 3b, e), which
and root turnover in grassland ecosystems (Bai et al.      gave the relatively deeper-rooted species (e.g., Ley-
2008). Nevertheless, it also has some limitations,         mus chinensis) more opportunity to access surface
such as edge effects and the depth of measurement          soil water and nutrients. More specifically, Leymus
being relatively shallow (Milchunas et al. 2009).          chinensis as the dominant species in the artificially
The root-window method cannot observe all roots            restored grassland would choose a conservative sur-
because some roots may grow in soil layers below           vival strategy, allocating more biomass to below-
the 0–20 cm profile. Despite that, Ma et al. (2020)        ground parts of the plant, which results in lower root
reported that nearly 70–90% of the roots in Songnen        turnover (Fig. 4) and longer root lifespan (Fig. 5a)

0–20 cm soil layers, indicating that our results are       restored grassland (Moreno-Gutierrez et al. 2012).
likely to be representative.                                  In addition, we found that mowing had contrasting
   Root production and root turnover are strongly          effects on root production and root turnover in different
affected by seeding (Wei et al. 2019; Assefa et al.        grassland types. In the artificially restored grassland, the
2017; Kotowska et al. 2016; Neigebauer et al. 2000).       interactive effects of mowing and seeding significantly
In this study, we found that seeding in the artificially   increased root production and root mortality (Fig. 3a,
restored grassland with its fewer species resulted in      b, d and e). These results are consistent with a previous
less root production under no mowing conditions            study using the ingrowth donut method where below-
(Fig. 3a, d). These results are in line with the idea      ground net primary productivity (BNPP) increased fol-
reported in the literature that root production is lower   lowing biomass removal (Diabate et al. 2018). Unfortu-
in species-poor plant communities than in species-         nately, we cannot compare the specific values estimated
rich plant communities (Mommer et al. 2010). In gen-       by the root window method to those generated by the

to root biomass productivity per area in the ingrowth           to aboveground or belowground parts based on resource
donut method (McCormack et al. 2014). The increase              constraints. Therefore, the improvement of soil conditions
in root production and root mortality following mowing          led to greater distribution of photosynthates to above-
may have been caused by a combination of several fac-           ground parts and reduced root turnover rate (Fig. 4) and C
tors. First, mowing led to substantial quantities of N being    allocation to roots. These results suggest that mowing and
removed from the grassland ecosystems. This would               seeding should be considered as effective and sustainable
reduce N availability in soils, which forces plants to allo-    measures to be implemented when restoring abandoned
cate more photosynthetic products to belowground organs         grassland.
(Bai et al. 2015). The photosynthetic products were used
to build additional fine roots and increase root lifespan       Conclusions
in the 10–20 cm soil profile, and this is likely a mecha-
nism for improving uptake of soil nutrients and water           Mowing effects on root production and root turnover dif-
(Fig. 5b; Frank 2002). Second, the reduction in leaf mate-      fered greatly between restored and artificial grassland.
rial by mowing greatly diminished transpiration, leading        Mowing significantly improved root production, root
to lower soil water consumption (Diabate et al. 2018)           turnover and decreased root lifespan in the artificially
and an increase in root production (Bai et al. 2010). Our       restored grassland due to an improvement in above-
regression analyses also revealed that fine root produc-        ground biomass and soil moisture. Hence, mowing
tion is positively correlated with aboveground biomass          accelerates belowground restoration in the artificially
and soil water content (Figs. 6 and 7), supporting this         restored grassland. In contrast, it is likely that these sig-
proposition. The third factor is that the loss of above-        nificant effects were not found in the naturally restored
ground parts after mowing would also speed up the death         grassland due to its greater species diversity. Our find-
of fine roots to avoid consumption of C in maintaining          ings highlight that appropriate management strategies,
old roots (Fig. 3b, e). This agrees with Wei et al. (2019)      i.e., optimal mowing and seeding, should be considered
who found that mowing significantly increased cumula-           during restoration of abandoned and degraded grasslands
tive root mortality in the 0–50 cm soil profile. Further-       from the perspective of belowground production. These
more, we found that the interactive effects of mowing           findings can provide a useful dataset for modeling root
and seeding significantly increased root turnover (Fig. 4).     dynamics in grassland systems. However, the estima-
This was mainly due to higher root production and an            tion of root may be biased within one method. Therefore,
unchanged root mean standing crop (Fig. 3a, c, d and f).        comparison with other methods at the same site includ-

through rapid turnover was beneficial for soil nutrient         lation modeling are required for improving the accuracy
cycling and plant compensatory growth (Fig. 7; Sims and         of data in the future study.
Singh 1978; Frank 2002). In contrast, the effect of mow-
ing on fine root production and root turnover was not sig-      Acknowledgements This work was financially supported by

Terms and Conditions
Springer Nature journal content, brought to you courtesy of Springer Nature Customer Service Center GmbH (“Springer Nature”).
Springer Nature supports a reasonable amount of sharing of research papers by authors, subscribers and authorised users (“Users”),
for small-scale personal, non-commercial use provided that all copyright, trade and service marks and other proprietary notices are
maintained. By accessing, sharing, receiving or otherwise using the Springer Nature journal content you agree to these terms of use
(“Terms”). For these purposes, Springer Nature considers academic use (by researchers and students) to be non-commercial.
These Terms are supplementary and will apply in addition to any applicable website terms and conditions, a relevant site licence or
a personal subscription. These Terms will prevail over any conflict or ambiguity with regards to the relevant terms, a site licence or
a personal subscription (to the extent of the conflict or ambiguity only). For Creative Commons-licensed articles, the terms of the
Creative Commons license used will apply.
We collect and use personal data to provide access to the Springer Nature journal content. We may also use these personal data
internally within ResearchGate and Springer Nature and as agreed share it, in an anonymised way, for purposes of tracking,
analysis and reporting. We will not otherwise disclose your personal data outside the ResearchGate or the Springer Nature group of
companies unless we have your permission as detailed in the Privacy Policy.
While Users may use the Springer Nature journal content for small scale, personal non-commercial use, it is important to note that
Users may not:

 1. use such content for the purpose of providing other users with access on a regular or large scale basis or as a means to
    circumvent access control;
 2. use such content where to do so would be considered a criminal or statutory offence in any jurisdiction, or gives rise to civil
    liability, or is otherwise unlawful;
 3. falsely or misleadingly imply or suggest endorsement, approval , sponsorship, or association unless explicitly agreed to by
    Springer Nature in writing;
 4. use bots or other automated methods to access the content or redirect messages
 5. override any security feature or exclusionary protocol; or
 6. share the content in order to create substitute for Springer Nature products or services or a systematic database of Springer
    Nature journal content.
In line with the restriction against commercial use, Springer Nature does not permit the creation of a product or service that creates
revenue, royalties, rent or income from our content or its inclusion as part of a paid for service or for other commercial gain.
Springer Nature journal content cannot be used for inter-library loans and librarians may not upload Springer Nature journal
content on a large scale into their, or any other, institutional repository.
These terms of use are reviewed regularly and may be amended at any time. Springer Nature is not obligated to publish any
information or content on this website and may remove it or features or functionality at our sole discretion, at any time with or
without notice. Springer Nature may revoke this licence to you at any time and remove access to any copies of the Springer Nature
journal content which have been saved.
To the fullest extent permitted by law, Springer Nature makes no warranties, representations or guarantees to Users, either express
or implied with respect to the Springer nature journal content and all parties disclaim and waive any implied warranties or
warranties imposed by law, including merchantability or fitness for any particular purpose.
Please note that these rights do not automatically extend to content, data or other material published by Springer Nature that may be
licensed from third parties.
If you would like to use or distribute our Springer Nature journal content to a wider audience or on a regular basis or in any other
manner not expressly permitted by these Terms, please contact Springer Nature at

onlineservice@springernature.com
